name: Kete (SS-369)
events: This page dealing with the wartime history of USS Kete was created in December 2025.
[No deck logs of this submarine are currently available to us so therefore some details, particularly regarding trials and exercises, might be missing on this page.]
[As USS Kete was lost with all hands while on her second war patrol details for this patrol are not known.]
31 Jul 1944 For the next ten days, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted builders trails and a short training programme on Lake Michigan.

20 Aug 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) departed Manitowoc for New Orleans via Chicago. From Chicago, down the Illinois and Mississipi Rivers, to New Orleans the passage was made in a floating dry dock.
28 Aug 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) arrived at New Orleans from Manitowoc.
31 Aug 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) departed New Orleans for the Panama Canal Zone.
Total bombing and attack restrictions were in place, 30 miles ahead, 60 miles behind and 15 miles on either side.
Escort was to be provided until point 'C' by USS PC-1241 (Lt. E.E. Weingarden, USNR). The remainder of the passage was to be made unescorted. (1)
5 Sep 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) arrived at Coco Solo in the Panama Canal Zone from New Orleans.
9 Sep 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) transited the Panama Canal westbound and arrived at Balboa.
10 Sep 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN). (2)
11 Sep 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN). (2)
12 Sep 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN). (2)
13 Sep 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN). (2)
14 Sep 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN). (3)
15 Sep 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN). (3)

16 Sep 1944 USS Dragonet (T/Cdr. J.H. Lewis, USN) and USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-713 (Lt.(jg) E.J. Spitz, USNR) and USS SC-986 (Lt.(jg) O.W. Scott, USNR). (3)

17 Sep 1944 USS Dragonet (T/Cdr. J.H. Lewis, USN) and USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-713 (Lt.(jg) E.J. Spitz, USNR) and USS SC-986 (Lt.(jg) O.W. Scott, USNR). (3)

18 Sep 1944 USS Dragonet (T/Cdr. J.H. Lewis, USN) and USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-713 (Lt.(jg) E.J. Spitz, USNR) and USS SC-986 (Lt.(jg) O.W. Scott, USNR). (3)

19 Sep 1944 USS Dragonet (T/Cdr. J.H. Lewis, USN) and USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN), USS SC-713 (Lt.(jg) E.J. Spitz, USNR) and USS SC-986 (Lt.(jg) O.W. Scott, USNR). (3)
20 Sep 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN) and USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN). (3)

21 Sep 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN), USS SC-713 (Lt.(jg) E.J. Spitz, USNR) and USS SC-986 (Lt.(jg) O.W. Scott, USNR). (3)
28 Sep 1944 USS Blenny (T/Cdr. W.H. Hazzard, USN) and USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) departed Balboa, Panama Canal Zone for Pearl Harbor.
15 Oct 1944 USS Blenny (T/Cdr. W.H. Hazzard, USN) and USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) arrived at Pearl Harbor from Balboa, Panama Canal Zone.

20 Oct 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) and USS Pipefish (T/Cdr. W.N. Deragon, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Connolly (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Collier, USNR) and USS Ramsay (Lt. R.R. Pollard, Jr., USNR).

21 Oct 1944 From 21 to 23 October 1944, USS Archerfish (T/Cdr. J.F. Enright, USN), USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) and USS Sea Cat (T/Cdr. R.R. McGregor, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with an inbound convoy coming from San Diego. This convoy was made up of the escort carriers USS Makin Island (T/Capt. W.B. Whaley, Jr., USN), USS Lunga Point (T/Capt. G.A.T. Washburn, USN), USS Bismarck Sea (T/Capt. J.L. Pratt, USN) and USS Salamaua (T/Capt. J.I. Taylor, Jr., USN). They were escorted since departure from San Diego by the destroyer escort USS Tills (Lt.Cdr. J.L. Brooks, USNR). The escort had been reinforced by the destroyer escorts USS Connolly (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Collier, USNR) and USS Bright (Lt.Cdr. W.A. McMahan, USNR) which had escorted the three submarines from Pearl Harbor to the rendezvous point with the incoming convoy.
25 Oct 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
26 Oct 1944 During 26/27 October 1944, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor. These included night exercises.

31 Oct 1944 Around 1330 VW/31, USS Sealion (ii) (T/Cdr. E.T. Reich, USN) and USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) departed from Pearl Harbor for their 3rd and 1st war patrols respectively. They were ordered to patrol in the East China Sea.
Both were to proceed to Midway first to top off with fuel and provisions and to make voyage repairs if needed. (4)
4 Nov 1944 Around 0800 Y/4 - 0830 Y/4, USS Sealion (ii) (T/Cdr. E.T. Reich, USN) and USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) arrived at Midway from Pearl Harbor.
Both departed for their patrol area around 1530 Y/4 after having topped off with fuel. One crew member of USS Kete, which was suffering from chronic sea sickness, was replaced at Midway. (4)

11 Nov 1944 At 1607 LM/11, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) received a contact report on an enemy patrol craft in position 30°10'N, 148°40'E, from USS Sealion (ii) (T/Cdr. E.T. Reich, USN). USS Kete set course to close but no contact was made.

At 1927 LM/11, rendezvous was made with USS Sealion (ii) and information was exchanged. It was decided to continue the passage to the patrol area instead of attacking the now alerted enemy patrol vessels again [USS Sealion had sighted two enemy patrol vessels/picket boats and had attacked one of the with gunfire but she was forced to break off the action due to accurate enemy return fire]. (5)

13 Nov 1944 At 0320 L/13, north-north-west of Chichi Jima, in position 29°18'N, 140°47'E, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) obtained radar contact bearing 180° at a range of 14000 yards. Two small vessels tracked on course 350° at a speed of 5 knots. Seas were rough due to a force 6-7 wind which precluded a successful torpedo or gun attack so T/Cdr. Rutter diced to avoid these vessels, thought to be enemy patrol boats, and continue the passage to the patrol area.

At 0435 L/13, in position 29°18'N, 140°39'E, another radar contact was obtained bearing 271° at a range of 13150 yards. One small vessel tracked on course 305° at a speed of 5 knots. This too was thought to be an enemy patrol vessel and avoiding action was taken.

At 0636 L/13, in position 29°12'N, 140°08'E, yet another radar contact was obtained bearing 290° at 7600 yards. Radar interference was also picked up. A small vessel tracked on course 090° at 12 knots. The radar interference appeared to be friendly. USS Kete turned away. The contact also turned away shortly afterwards and proceeded on course 270° as if for an end around. It was attempted to exchange calls with the SJ radar but there came no reply. It was have been a radar equipped Japanese patrol vessel but may also have been a friendly submarine. USS Kete soon resumed course to continue the passage to her patrol area. (5)

15 Nov 1944 At 0020 KL/15, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) exchanged calls with the SJ radar with USS Billfish (T/Cdr. V.C. Turner, USN) which had left patrol the previous evening and was en-route to Midway. (5)

15 Nov 1944 At 0321 KL/15, south-east of Tanega Shima, in position 29°36'N, 132°00'E, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) obtained radar contact bearing 207° at a range of 5350 yards. The vessel tracked on a course of 090° at a speed of 13 knots. It was thought to be a small vessel and was avoided. USS Kete continued her passage to her patrol area. (5)

16 Nov 1944 At 1600 K/15, south of Saishu Island [now called Jeju Island], in position 31°56'N, 126°43'E, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) sighted a small sampan bearing 214° at a range of 14000 yards. It was avoided and USS Kete continued passage to her patrol area where she would arrive the following morning. (5)
17 Nov 1944 At 0748 K/17, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) dived in her patrol area for submerged patrol.

At 0922 K/17, the bow hydroplanes were out of commission. The entire system was checked. It was reluctantly decided that it was most likely the same problem that had forced other submarines to abandon their patrols. It was decided to retire to a 'quiet area' in the evening to breakdown the cylinder and investigate.
At 1758 K/17, USS Kete surfaced and commenced to retire from the area. (5)

20 Nov 1944 At 2250 KL/20, after a few days during which repairs were attempted, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) left patrol to proceed to Saipan to effect repairs as had been ordered by COMSUBSPAC. Her bow hydroplanes were operative in an emergency position only if required. (5)

23 Nov 1944 At 2333 KL/23, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) exchanged calls with the SJ radar with the westbound USS Blenny (T/Cdr. W.H. Hazzard, USN) which had left Saipan for patrol earlier that day. (5)

24 Nov 1944 Around 1230 KL/24, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) arrived at Saipan to repair her malfunctioning bow planes. She was taken in had for repairs by USS Fulton (T/Capt. A.A. Clarkson, USN).
USS Kete was escorted in by USS Fanning (T/Cdr. J.C. Bentley, USN) which had joined around 0635 KL/24.
23 Dec 1944 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) conducted post repair trials off Saipan. The trials were completed satisfactory. (5)
24 Dec 1944 Around 1330 K/24, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) departed from Saipan to resume her first war patrol. She was to patrol in the East China Sea off the Ryukyu Islands.
USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) was in company until 2300 K/25. USS Kraken was to patrol in the South China Sea, which was also her first war patrol.
They were escorted out until 1900 K/24 by USS PC-1126 (Lt. J.F. Carrere, USNR). (6)
28 Dec 1944 Around 1100 K/28, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) entered her patrol area. (5)

28 Dec 1944 At 2007 K/28, east-north-east of Okinawa, in position 26°55'N, 129°09'E, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) exchanged calls and information with the SJ radar with USS Sea Devil (T/Cdr. R.E. Styles, USN) which was due to leave patrol the following day. (7)
29 Dec 1944 At 0210 K/29, south-east of Toku-no Shima, in approximate position 27°23'N, 129°20'E, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) exchanged calls with USS Whale (T/Cdr. J.B. Grady, USN). (5)
1 Jan 1945 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) received orders to perform lifeguard duties off Okinawa. Course was set to comply with these orders. (5)
2 Jan 1945 At 0130 K/2, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) exchanged calls with USS Sea Fox (T/Cdr. R.C. Klinker, USN). (5)
3 Jan 1945 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) searched for a reported downed aviator close inshore to the south of Okinawa. He could however not be found. (5)

9 Jan 1945 At 2344 I/9, near Okinawa, USS Tinosa (T/Cdr. R.C. Latham, USN) obtained SJ radar interference bearing 020° which was believed to be coming from USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) but there came no reply to the challenges sent with the SJ radar. (8)
10 Jan 1945 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) is released from lifeguard duties and resumed regular patrol. (5)
21 Jan 1945 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) is again assigned to lifeguard duties off Okinawa. (5)
24 Jan 1945 USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) resumed regular patrol, having been released from lifeguard duties. (5)
27 Jan 1945 At 0630 K/27, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) left patrol to proceed to Guam. (5)
30 Jan 1945 Around 1300 K/30, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) ended her 1st war patrol at Guam.
She was escorted in by USS John. D. Henley (T/Cdr. C.H. Smith, USN) which had joined around 0730 K/30.
At Guam, USS Kete was taken in hand for refit by USS Sperry (T/Capt. J.P. Thew, USN). On the departure of the tender from Guam, USS Proteus (T/Capt. C.N. Day, USN) took over.
4 Feb 1945 USS Kete (?) is docked at Guam. [We have been unable to find out when she was undocked.] (9)

19 Feb 1945 During 19 and 20 February 1945, USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) conducted exercises off Guam with USS Doherty (Lt. W.J. O'Brien, USNR), USS Baron (Lt.Cdr. W.M. Dickerson, USNR) and USS Greenlet (Lt.Cdr. J.T. Hodgson, Jr., USNR). These included night exercises. (10)

21 Feb 1945 USS Sawfish (T/Lt.Cdr. D.H. Pugh, USN) and USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) conducted exercises off Guam with USS Doherty (Lt. W.J. O'Brien, USNR), USS Baron (Lt.Cdr. W.M. Dickerson, USNR) and USS Greenlet (Lt.Cdr. J.T. Hodgson, Jr., USNR). (10)

23 Feb 1945 During 23 and 24 February 1945, USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) conducted exercises off Guam with USS Greenlet (Lt.Cdr. J.T. Hodgson, Jr., USNR). These included night exercises. (10)
25 Feb 1945 USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) and USS Sea Fox (T/Cdr. R.C. Klinker, USN) conducted exercises off Guam with USS Greenlet (Lt.Cdr. J.T. Hodgson, Jr., USNR). (10)
1 Mar 1945 USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) departed from Guam for her 2nd war patrol, and was ordered to patrol of the Ryukyu Islands.
First she was to proceed to Saipan escorted for part of the way by USS Roe (T/Cdr. J.P. Craft, Jr., USN).
Both USS Kete and USS Roe arrived at Saipan later the same day. (11)
2 Mar 1945 Around 1000 K/2, USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) departed Saipan to proceed to her patrol area.
She was escorted out until around 1600 K/2, by USS Roe (T/Cdr. J.P. Craft, Jr., USN). (11)

5 Mar 1945 At 0420 I/5, USS Scabbardfish (T/Cdr. F.A. Gunn, USN), which was enroute from her patrol area to Saipan, encountered SJ radar interference from a US submarine proceeding in the opposite direction. It was attempted to exchange recognition signals but there came no reply. It was thought the other submarine was USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) which was expected to be in the area en-route to her patrol area.
[As USS Kete was lost on this patrol there is no patrol report available for this submarine.] (12)

7 Mar 1945 At 2135 K/7, south-east of Nakanoshima, in position 29°27'N, 130°06'E, USS Tilefish (T/Cdr. W.F. Schlech, Jr., USN) encountered USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN). Recognition signals were exchanged.
[As USS Kete was lost on this patrol there is no patrol report available for this submarine.] (13)

10 Mar 1945 USS Kete (T/Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) attacked a Japanese convoy. This was convoy Kana 803 which was made up of the transports Dokan Maru (2270 GRT, built 1944), Keizan Maru (2308 GRT, built 1918) and Sanka Maru (2488 GRT, built 1918). It was on passage from Kagoshima, Kyushu, Japan to Naha, Okinawa and was escorted by the frigates Kaibokan 44, Kaibokan 118, auxiliary minesweeper Taihei Maru No.3 (197 GRT, built 1930) and auxiliary netlayer Shinto Maru No.2 (540 GRT, built 1944) (all links are offsite links). The attacks took place about 100 nautical miles north-west of Amami o Shima in approximate position 29°48'N, 128°02'E and USS Kete managed to sink all three transports in two attack runs.
That night, USS Kete reported in a signal, sent from position 29°31'N, 127°55'E, that she had sunk three medium sized enemy freighters.

14 Mar 1945 USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) reported that she had fired four torpedoes but missed a small Japanese cable laying type of vessel. She also reported that she has only three torpedoes left.

USS Kete, which was to have remained on patrol until sunset of 7 April was now ordered to leave patrol on March 20th and proceed to Pearl Harbor via Midway. This due to the small number of torpedoes remaining on board.
19 Mar 1945 USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) acknowledged the receipt of the orders to leave her patrol area on the next day.
20 Mar 1945 USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) sent a special weather report. This is the last ever heard from her.
The report was sent from position 29°38'N, 130°02'E.
31 Mar 1945 On or around this date, USS Kete (T/Lt.Cdr. E. Ackerman, USN) was expected to arrive at Midway. Several attempts to raise her by radio remained unanswered.
